sourceRefs.fallbackText: In the following examples, input and output are distinguished by the presence or absence of prompts ( >>> and … ): to repeat the example, you must type everything after the prompt, when the prompt appears; lines that do not begin with a prompt are output from the interpreter. Note that a secondary prompt on a line by itself in an example means you must type
sourceRefs.textQuote: In the following examples, input and output are distinguished by the presence or absence of prompts ( >>> and … ): to repeat the example, you must type everything after the prompt, when the prompt appears; lines that do

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You can use the “Copy” button (it appears in the upper-right corner when hovering over or tapping a code example), which strips prompts and omits output, to copy and paste the input lines into your interpreter.
sourceRefs.textQuote: You can use the “Copy” button (it appears in the upper-right corner when hovering over or tapping a code example), which strips prompts and omits output, to copy and paste the input lines into your interpreter.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Many of the examples in this manual, even those entered at the interactive prompt, include comments. Comments in Python start with the hash character, # , and extend to the end of the physical line. A comment may appear at the start of a line or following whitespace or code, but not within a string literal. A hash character within a string literal is just a
sourceRefs.textQuote: Many of the examples in this manual, even those entered at the interactive prompt, include comments. Comments in Python start with the hash character, # , and extend to the end of the physical line. A comment may appear

sourceRefs.fallbackText: # this is the first comment spam = 1 # and this is the second comment # ... and now a third! text = "# This is not a comment because it's inside quotes."
sourceRefs.textQuote: # this is the first comment spam = 1 # and this is the second comment # ... and now a third! text = "# This is not a comment because it's inside quotes."

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Let’s try some simple Python commands. Start the interpreter and wait for the primary prompt, >>> . (It shouldn’t take long.)
sourceRefs.textQuote: Let’s try some simple Python commands. Start the interpreter and wait for the primary prompt, >>> . (It shouldn’t take long.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The interpreter acts as a simple calculator: you can type an expression into it and it will write the value. Expression syntax is straightforward: the operators + , - , * and / can be used to perform arithmetic; parentheses ( () ) can be used for grouping. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The interpreter acts as a simple calculator: you can type an expression into it and it will write the value. Expression syntax is straightforward: the operators + , - , * and / can be used to perform arithmetic; parenthe

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> 2 + 2 4 >>> 50 - 5 * 6 20 >>> ( 50 - 5 * 6 ) / 4 5.0 >>> 8 / 5 # division always returns a floating-point number 1.6
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> 2 + 2 4 >>> 50 - 5 * 6 20 >>> ( 50 - 5 * 6 ) / 4 5.0 >>> 8 / 5 # division always returns a floating-point number 1.6

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The integer numbers (e.g. 2 , 4 , 20 ) have type int , the ones with a fractional part (e.g. 5.0 , 1.6 ) have type float . We will see more about numeric types later in the tutorial.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The integer numbers (e.g. 2 , 4 , 20 ) have type int , the ones with a fractional part (e.g. 5.0 , 1.6 ) have type float . We will see more about numeric types later in the tutorial.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Division ( / ) always returns a float. To do floor division and get an integer result you can use the // operator; to calculate the remainder you can use % :
sourceRefs.textQuote: Division ( / ) always returns a float. To do floor division and get an integer result you can use the // operator; to calculate the remainder you can use % :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> 17 / 3 # classic division returns a float 5.666666666666667 >>> >>> 17 // 3 # floor division discards the fractional part 5 >>> 17 % 3 # the % operator returns the remainder of the division 2 >>> 5 * 3 + 2 # floored quotient * divisor + remainder 17
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> 17 / 3 # classic division returns a float 5.666666666666667 >>> >>> 17 // 3 # floor division discards the fractional part 5 >>> 17 % 3 # the % operator returns the remainder of the division 2 >>> 5 * 3 + 2 # floored

sourceRefs.fallbackText: With Python, it is possible to use the ** operator to calculate powers [ 1 ] :
sourceRefs.textQuote: With Python, it is possible to use the ** operator to calculate powers [ 1 ] :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> 5 ** 2 # 5 squared 25 >>> 2 ** 7 # 2 to the power of 7 128
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> 5 ** 2 # 5 squared 25 >>> 2 ** 7 # 2 to the power of 7 128

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The equal sign ( = ) is used to assign a value to a variable. Afterwards, no result is displayed before the next interactive prompt:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The equal sign ( = ) is used to assign a value to a variable. Afterwards, no result is displayed before the next interactive prompt:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> width = 20 >>> height = 5 * 9 >>> width * height 900
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> width = 20 >>> height = 5 * 9 >>> width * height 900

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If a variable is not “defined” (assigned a value), trying to use it will give you an error:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If a variable is not “defined” (assigned a value), trying to use it will give you an error:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> n # try to access an undefined variable Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> NameError : name 'n' is not defined
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> n # try to access an undefined variable Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> NameError : name 'n' is not defined

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There is full support for floating point; operators with mixed type operands convert the integer operand to floating point:
sourceRefs.textQuote: There is full support for floating point; operators with mixed type operands convert the integer operand to floating point:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In interactive mode, the last printed expression is assigned to the variable _ . This means that when you are using Python as a desk calculator, it is somewhat easier to continue calculations, for example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: In interactive mode, the last printed expression is assigned to the variable _ . This means that when you are using Python as a desk calculator, it is somewhat easier to continue calculations, for example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This variable should be treated as read-only by the user. Don’t explicitly assign a value to it — you would create an independent local variable with the same name masking the built-in variable with its magic behavior.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This variable should be treated as read-only by the user. Don’t explicitly assign a value to it — you would create an independent local variable with the same name masking the built-in variable with its magic behavior.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In addition to int and float , Python supports other types of numbers, such as Decimal and Fraction . Python also has built-in support for complex numbers , and uses the j or J suffix to indicate the imaginary part (e.g. 3+5j ).
sourceRefs.textQuote: In addition to int and float , Python supports other types of numbers, such as Decimal and Fraction . Python also has built-in support for complex numbers , and uses the j or J suffix to indicate the imaginary part (e.g.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Python can manipulate text (represented by type str , so-called “strings”) as well as numbers. This includes characters “ ! ”, words “ rabbit ”, names “ Paris ”, sentences “ Got your back. ”, etc. “ Yay! :) ”. They can be enclosed in single quotes ( '...' ) or double quotes ( "..." ) with the same result [ 2 ] .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Python can manipulate text (represented by type str , so-called “strings”) as well as numbers. This includes characters “ ! ”, words “ rabbit ”, names “ Paris ”, sentences “ Got your back. ”, etc. “ Yay! :) ”. They can b

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> 'spam eggs' # single quotes 'spam eggs' >>> "Paris rabbit got your back :)! Yay!" # double quotes 'Paris rabbit got your back :)! Yay!' >>> '1975' # digits and numerals enclosed in quotes are also strings '1975'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> 'spam eggs' # single quotes 'spam eggs' >>> "Paris rabbit got your back :)! Yay!" # double quotes 'Paris rabbit got your back :)! Yay!' >>> '1975' # digits and numerals enclosed in quotes are also strings '1975'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To quote a quote, we need to “escape” it, by preceding it with \ . Alternatively, we can use the other type of quotation marks:
sourceRefs.textQuote: To quote a quote, we need to “escape” it, by preceding it with \ . Alternatively, we can use the other type of quotation marks:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> 'doesn \' t' # use \' to escape the single quote... "doesn't" >>> "doesn't" # ...or use double quotes instead "doesn't" >>> '"Yes," they said.' '"Yes," they said.' >>> " \" Yes, \" they said." '"Yes," they said.' >>> '"Isn \' t," they said.' '"Isn\'t," they said.'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> 'doesn \' t' # use \' to escape the single quote... "doesn't" >>> "doesn't" # ...or use double quotes instead "doesn't" >>> '"Yes," they said.' '"Yes," they said.' >>> " \" Yes, \" they said." '"Yes," they said.' >>>

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In the Python shell, the string definition and output string can look different. The print() function produces a more readable output, by omitting the enclosing quotes and by printing escaped and special characters:
sourceRefs.textQuote: In the Python shell, the string definition and output string can look different. The print() function produces a more readable output, by omitting the enclosing quotes and by printing escaped and special characters:

sourceRefs.fallbackText:
>>> s = 'First line. 
 Second line.' # 
 means newline >>> s # without print(), special characters are included in the string 'First line.
Second line.' >>> print ( s ) # with print(), special characters are interpreted, so 
 produces new line First line. Second line.
sourceRefs.textQuote:
>>> s = 'First line. 
 Second line.' # 
 means newline >>> s # without print(), special characters are included in the string 'First line.
Second line.' >>> print ( s ) # with print(), special characters are interpret

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you don’t want characters prefaced by \ to be interpreted as special characters, you can use raw strings by adding an r before the first quote:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you don’t want characters prefaced by \ to be interpreted as special characters, you can use raw strings by adding an r before the first quote:

sourceRefs.fallbackText:
>>> print ( 'C: 	 his 
 ame' ) # here 	 means tab, 
 means newline C: his ame >>> print ( r 'C:	his
ame' ) # note the r before the quote C:	his
ame
sourceRefs.textQuote:
>>> print ( 'C: 	 his 
 ame' ) # here 	 means tab, 
 means newline C: his ame >>> print ( r 'C:	his
ame' ) # note the r before the quote C:	his
ame

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There is one subtle aspect to raw strings: a raw string may not end in an odd number of \ characters; see the FAQ entry for more information and workarounds.
sourceRefs.textQuote: There is one subtle aspect to raw strings: a raw string may not end in an odd number of \ characters; see the FAQ entry for more information and workarounds.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: String literals can span multiple lines. One way is using triple-quotes: """...""" or '''...''' . End-of-line characters are automatically included in the string, but it’s possible to prevent this by adding a \ at the end of the line. In the following example, the initial newline is not included:
sourceRefs.textQuote: String literals can span multiple lines. One way is using triple-quotes: """...""" or '''...''' . End-of-line characters are automatically included in the string, but it’s possible to prevent this by adding a \ at the en

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> print ( """ \ ... Usage: thingy [OPTIONS] ... -h Display this usage message ... -H hostname Hostname to connect to ... """ ) Usage: thingy [OPTIONS] -h Display this usage message -H hostname Hostname to connect to >>>
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> print ( """ \ ... Usage: thingy [OPTIONS] ... -h Display this usage message ... -H hostname Hostname to connect to ... """ ) Usage: thingy [OPTIONS] -h Display this usage message -H hostname Hostname to connect to >>

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Strings can be concatenated (glued together) with the + operator, and repeated with * :
sourceRefs.textQuote: Strings can be concatenated (glued together) with the + operator, and repeated with * :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> # 3 times 'un', followed by 'ium' >>> 3 * 'un' + 'ium' 'unununium'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> # 3 times 'un', followed by 'ium' >>> 3 * 'un' + 'ium' 'unununium'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Two or more string literals (i.e. the ones enclosed between quotes) next to each other are automatically concatenated.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Two or more string literals (i.e. the ones enclosed between quotes) next to each other are automatically concatenated.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> 'Py' 'thon' 'Python'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> 'Py' 'thon' 'Python'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This feature is particularly useful when you want to break long strings:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This feature is particularly useful when you want to break long strings:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> text = ( 'Put several strings within parentheses ' ... 'to have them joined together.' ) >>> text 'Put several strings within parentheses to have them joined together.'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> text = ( 'Put several strings within parentheses ' ... 'to have them joined together.' ) >>> text 'Put several strings within parentheses to have them joined together.'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This only works with two literals though, not with variables or expressions:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This only works with two literals though, not with variables or expressions:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> prefix = 'Py' >>> prefix 'thon' # can't concatenate a variable and a string literal File "<stdin>" , line 1 prefix 'thon' ^^^^^^ SyntaxError : invalid syntax >>> ( 'un' * 3 ) 'ium' File "<stdin>" , line 1 ( 'un' * 3 ) 'ium' ^^^^^ SyntaxError : invalid syntax
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> prefix = 'Py' >>> prefix 'thon' # can't concatenate a variable and a string literal File "<stdin>" , line 1 prefix 'thon' ^^^^^^ SyntaxError : invalid syntax >>> ( 'un' * 3 ) 'ium' File "<stdin>" , line 1 ( 'un' * 3

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you want to concatenate variables or a variable and a literal, use + :
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you want to concatenate variables or a variable and a literal, use + :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> prefix + 'thon' 'Python'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> prefix + 'thon' 'Python'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Strings can be indexed (subscripted), with the first character having index 0. There is no separate character type; a character is simply a string of size one:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Strings can be indexed (subscripted), with the first character having index 0. There is no separate character type; a character is simply a string of size one:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> word = 'Python' >>> word [ 0 ] # character in position 0 'P' >>> word [ 5 ] # character in position 5 'n'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> word = 'Python' >>> word [ 0 ] # character in position 0 'P' >>> word [ 5 ] # character in position 5 'n'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Indices may also be negative numbers, to start counting from the right:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Indices may also be negative numbers, to start counting from the right:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> word [ - 1 ] # last character 'n' >>> word [ - 2 ] # second-last character 'o' >>> word [ - 6 ] 'P'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> word [ - 1 ] # last character 'n' >>> word [ - 2 ] # second-last character 'o' >>> word [ - 6 ] 'P'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that since -0 is the same as 0, negative indices start from -1.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note that since -0 is the same as 0, negative indices start from -1.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In addition to indexing, slicing is also supported. While indexing is used to obtain individual characters, slicing allows you to obtain a substring:
sourceRefs.textQuote: In addition to indexing, slicing is also supported. While indexing is used to obtain individual characters, slicing allows you to obtain a substring:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> word [ 0 : 2 ] # characters from position 0 (included) to 2 (excluded) 'Py' >>> word [ 2 : 5 ] # characters from position 2 (included) to 5 (excluded) 'tho'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> word [ 0 : 2 ] # characters from position 0 (included) to 2 (excluded) 'Py' >>> word [ 2 : 5 ] # characters from position 2 (included) to 5 (excluded) 'tho'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Slice indices have useful defaults; an omitted first index defaults to zero, an omitted second index defaults to the size of the string being sliced.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Slice indices have useful defaults; an omitted first index defaults to zero, an omitted second index defaults to the size of the string being sliced.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> word [: 2 ] # character from the beginning to position 2 (excluded) 'Py' >>> word [ 4 :] # characters from position 4 (included) to the end 'on' >>> word [ - 2 :] # characters from the second-last (included) to the end 'on'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> word [: 2 ] # character from the beginning to position 2 (excluded) 'Py' >>> word [ 4 :] # characters from position 4 (included) to the end 'on' >>> word [ - 2 :] # characters from the second-last (included) to the e

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note how the start is always included, and the end always excluded. This makes sure that s[:i] + s[i:] is always equal to s :
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note how the start is always included, and the end always excluded. This makes sure that s[:i] + s[i:] is always equal to s :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> word [: 2 ] + word [ 2 :] 'Python' >>> word [: 4 ] + word [ 4 :] 'Python'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> word [: 2 ] + word [ 2 :] 'Python' >>> word [: 4 ] + word [ 4 :] 'Python'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One way to remember how slices work is to think of the indices as pointing between characters, with the left edge of the first character numbered 0. Then the right edge of the last character of a string of n characters has index n , for example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: One way to remember how slices work is to think of the indices as pointing between characters, with the left edge of the first character numbered 0. Then the right edge of the last character of a string of n characters h

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The first row of numbers gives the position of the indices 0…6 in the string; the second row gives the corresponding negative indices. The slice from i to j consists of all characters between the edges labeled i and j , respectively.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The first row of numbers gives the position of the indices 0…6 in the string; the second row gives the corresponding negative indices. The slice from i to j consists of all characters between the edges labeled i and j ,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For non-negative indices, the length of a slice is the difference of the indices, if both are within bounds. For example, the length of word[1:3] is 2.
sourceRefs.textQuote: For non-negative indices, the length of a slice is the difference of the indices, if both are within bounds. For example, the length of word[1:3] is 2.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Attempting to use an index that is too large will result in an error:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Attempting to use an index that is too large will result in an error:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> word [ 42 ] # the word only has 6 characters Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> IndexError : string index out of range
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> word [ 42 ] # the word only has 6 characters Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> IndexError : string index out of range

sourceRefs.fallbackText: However, out of range slice indexes are handled gracefully when used for slicing:
sourceRefs.textQuote: However, out of range slice indexes are handled gracefully when used for slicing:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Python strings cannot be changed — they are immutable . Therefore, assigning to an indexed position in the string results in an error:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Python strings cannot be changed — they are immutable . Therefore, assigning to an indexed position in the string results in an error:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> word [ 0 ] = 'J' Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> TypeError : 'str' object does not support item assignment >>> word [ 2 :] = 'py' Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> TypeError : 'str' object does not support item assignment
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> word [ 0 ] = 'J' Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> TypeError : 'str' object does not support item assignment >>> word [ 2 :] = 'py' Traceback (most recent call last): File "<std

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you need a different string, you should create a new one:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you need a different string, you should create a new one:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> 'J' + word [ 1 :] 'Jython' >>> word [: 2 ] + 'py' 'Pypy'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> 'J' + word [ 1 :] 'Jython' >>> word [: 2 ] + 'py' 'Pypy'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The built-in function len() returns the length of a string:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The built-in function len() returns the length of a string:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> s = 'supercalifragilisticexpialidocious' >>> len ( s ) 34
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> s = 'supercalifragilisticexpialidocious' >>> len ( s ) 34

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Strings are examples of sequence types , and support the common operations supported by such types.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Strings are examples of sequence types , and support the common operations supported by such types.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Strings support a large number of methods for basic transformations and searching.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Strings support a large number of methods for basic transformations and searching.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: String literals that have embedded expressions.
sourceRefs.textQuote: String literals that have embedded expressions.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Information about string formatting with str.format() .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Information about string formatting with str.format() .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The old formatting operations invoked when strings are the left operand of the % operator are described in more detail here.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The old formatting operations invoked when strings are the left operand of the % operator are described in more detail here.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Python knows a number of compound data types, used to group together other values. The most versatile is the list , which can be written as a list of comma-separated values (items) between square brackets. Lists might contain items of different types, but usually the items all have the same type.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Python knows a number of compound data types, used to group together other values. The most versatile is the list , which can be written as a list of comma-separated values (items) between square brackets. Lists might co

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Like strings (and all other built-in sequence types), lists can be indexed and sliced:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Like strings (and all other built-in sequence types), lists can be indexed and sliced:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> squares [ 0 ] # indexing returns the item 1 >>> squares [ - 1 ] 25 >>> squares [ - 3 :] # slicing returns a new list [9, 16, 25]
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> squares [ 0 ] # indexing returns the item 1 >>> squares [ - 1 ] 25 >>> squares [ - 3 :] # slicing returns a new list [9, 16, 25]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Lists also support operations like concatenation:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Lists also support operations like concatenation:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unlike strings, which are immutable , lists are a mutable type, i.e. it is possible to change their content:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Unlike strings, which are immutable , lists are a mutable type, i.e. it is possible to change their content:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> cubes = [ 1 , 8 , 27 , 65 , 125 ] # something's wrong here >>> 4 ** 3 # the cube of 4 is 64, not 65! 64 >>> cubes [ 3 ] = 64 # replace the wrong value >>> cubes [1, 8, 27, 64, 125]
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> cubes = [ 1 , 8 , 27 , 65 , 125 ] # something's wrong here >>> 4 ** 3 # the cube of 4 is 64, not 65! 64 >>> cubes [ 3 ] = 64 # replace the wrong value >>> cubes [1, 8, 27, 64, 125]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You can also add new items at the end of the list, by using the list.append() method (we will see more about methods later):
sourceRefs.textQuote: You can also add new items at the end of the list, by using the list.append() method (we will see more about methods later):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> cubes . append ( 216 ) # add the cube of 6 >>> cubes . append ( 7 ** 3 ) # and the cube of 7 >>> cubes [1, 8, 27, 64, 125, 216, 343]
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> cubes . append ( 216 ) # add the cube of 6 >>> cubes . append ( 7 ** 3 ) # and the cube of 7 >>> cubes [1, 8, 27, 64, 125, 216, 343]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Simple assignment in Python never copies data. When you assign a list to a variable, the variable refers to the existing list . Any changes you make to the list through one variable will be seen through all other variables that refer to it.:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Simple assignment in Python never copies data. When you assign a list to a variable, the variable refers to the existing list . Any changes you make to the list through one variable will be seen through all other variabl

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> rgb = [ "Red" , "Green" , "Blue" ] >>> rgba = rgb >>> id ( rgb ) == id ( rgba ) # they reference the same object True >>> rgba . append ( "Alph" ) >>> rgb ["Red", "Green", "Blue", "Alph"]
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> rgb = [ "Red" , "Green" , "Blue" ] >>> rgba = rgb >>> id ( rgb ) == id ( rgba ) # they reference the same object True >>> rgba . append ( "Alph" ) >>> rgb ["Red", "Green", "Blue", "Alph"]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: All slice operations return a new list containing the requested elements. This means that the following slice returns a shallow copy of the list:
sourceRefs.textQuote: All slice operations return a new list containing the requested elements. This means that the following slice returns a shallow copy of the list:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> correct_rgba = rgba [:] >>> correct_rgba [ - 1 ] = "Alpha" >>> correct_rgba ["Red", "Green", "Blue", "Alpha"] >>> rgba ["Red", "Green", "Blue", "Alph"]
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> correct_rgba = rgba [:] >>> correct_rgba [ - 1 ] = "Alpha" >>> correct_rgba ["Red", "Green", "Blue", "Alpha"] >>> rgba ["Red", "Green", "Blue", "Alph"]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Assignment to slices is also possible, and this can even change the size of the list or clear it entirely:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Assignment to slices is also possible, and this can even change the size of the list or clear it entirely:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> letters = [ 'a' , 'b' , 'c' , 'd' , 'e' , 'f' , 'g' ] >>> letters ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g'] >>> # replace some values >>> letters [ 2 : 5 ] = [ 'C' , 'D' , 'E' ] >>> letters ['a', 'b', 'C', 'D', 'E', 'f', 'g'] >>> # now remove them >>> letters [ 2 : 5 ] = [] >>> letters ['a', 'b', 'f', 'g'] >>> # clear the list by replacing all the elements wit
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> letters = [ 'a' , 'b' , 'c' , 'd' , 'e' , 'f' , 'g' ] >>> letters ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g'] >>> # replace some values >>> letters [ 2 : 5 ] = [ 'C' , 'D' , 'E' ] >>> letters ['a', 'b', 'C', 'D', 'E', 'f', '

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The built-in function len() also applies to lists:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The built-in function len() also applies to lists:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> letters = [ 'a' , 'b' , 'c' , 'd' ] >>> len ( letters ) 4
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> letters = [ 'a' , 'b' , 'c' , 'd' ] >>> len ( letters ) 4

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is possible to nest lists (create lists containing other lists), for example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Of course, we can use Python for more complicated tasks than adding two and two together. For instance, we can write an initial sub-sequence of the Fibonacci series as follows:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Of course, we can use Python for more complicated tasks than adding two and two together. For instance, we can write an initial sub-sequence of the Fibonacci series as follows:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> # Fibonacci series: >>> # the sum of two elements defines the next >>> a , b = 0 , 1 >>> while a < 10 : ... print ( a ) ... a , b = b , a + b ... 0 1 1 2 3 5 8
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> # Fibonacci series: >>> # the sum of two elements defines the next >>> a , b = 0 , 1 >>> while a < 10 : ... print ( a ) ... a , b = b , a + b ... 0 1 1 2 3 5 8

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This example introduces several new features.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This example introduces several new features.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The first line contains a multiple assignment : the variables a and b simultaneously get the new values 0 and 1. On the last line this is used again, demonstrating that the expressions on the right-hand side are all evaluated first before any of the assignments take place. The right-hand side expressions are evaluated from the left to the right.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The first line contains a multiple assignment : the variables a and b simultaneously get the new values 0 and 1. On the last line this is used again, demonstrating that the expressions on the right-hand side are all eval

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The while loop executes as long as the condition (here: a < 10 ) remains true. In Python, like in C, any non-zero integer value is true; zero is false. The condition may also be a string or list value, in fact any sequence; anything with a non-zero length is true, empty sequences are false. The test used in the example is a simple comparison. The standard co

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The body of the loop is indented : indentation is Python’s way of grouping statements. At the interactive prompt, you have to type a tab or space(s) for each indented line. In practice you will prepare more complicated input for Python with a text editor; all decent text editors have an auto-indent facility. When a compound statement is entered interactively
sourceRefs.textQuote: The body of the loop is indented : indentation is Python’s way of grouping statements. At the interactive prompt, you have to type a tab or space(s) for each indented line. In practice you will prepare more complicated i

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The print() function writes the value of the argument(s) it is given. It differs from just writing the expression you want to write (as we did earlier in the calculator examples) in the way it handles multiple arguments, floating-point quantities, and strings. Strings are printed without quotes, and a space is inserted between items, so you can format things

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> i = 256 * 256 >>> print ( 'The value of i is' , i ) The value of i is 65536
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> i = 256 * 256 >>> print ( 'The value of i is' , i ) The value of i is 65536

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Since ** has higher precedence than - , -3**2 will be interpreted as -(3**2) and thus result in -9 . To avoid this and get 9 , you can use (-3)**2 .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Since ** has higher precedence than - , -3**2 will be interpreted as -(3**2) and thus result in -9 . To avoid this and get 9 , you can use (-3)**2 .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unlike other languages, special characters such as \n have the same meaning with both single ( '...' ) and double ( "..." ) quotes. The only difference between the two is that within single quotes you don’t need to escape " (but you have to escape \' ) and vice versa.
